meta.title: NAT Online Check · Serverless
meta.description: Serverless NAT check using WebRTC + STUN.
hero.title: Identify Your NAT Type in One Click
hero.subtitle: Built on browser WebRTC and STUN. We analyze public mappings and port behavior locally. No personal data is uploaded.
summary.title: Summary
summary.note: Browser-only results may not fully distinguish some types.
details.title: Details
types.title: NAT Type Overview
types.nat1.title: NAT1 · Full Cone
types.nat1.desc: External hosts can reach the mapped address directly. Best for P2P.
types.nat2.title: NAT2 · Restricted Cone
types.nat2.desc: External hosts can respond only after your device initiates traffic.
types.nat3.title: NAT3 · Port Restricted
types.nat3.desc: Restricts both IP and port. P2P may be limited.
types.nat4.title: NAT4 · Symmetric
types.nat4.desc: Different destinations get different mappings. Hole punching is hard.
types.note: Browser-only tests cannot reliably distinguish NAT2 vs NAT3. We show “Cone NAT (NAT2/3)”.
knowledge.title: Understand NAT Behavior
knowledge.card1.title: Why NAT Affects Experience
knowledge.card1.body: Strong bandwidth does not guarantee good peer connectivity. NAT determines whether external traffic can reach your device. Strict mappings often force relays, increasing latency.
knowledge.card2.title: Common Causes
knowledge.card2.item1: Double NAT (modem + router both doing NAT)
knowledge.card3.title: Why Run This Test
knowledge.card3.body: Knowing your NAT type explains unstable connections and helps decide whether to enable UPnP, set port forwarding, or request a public IP from your ISP.
faq.title: FAQ
faq.a1: No. The test runs in your browser and only contacts public STUN servers.
faq.q2: Why does it say “Possibly symmetric NAT”?
faq.a2: If different STUN servers return different mapped ports, symmetric NAT is likely.
faq.q3: How can I improve my NAT type?
faq.a3: Try UPnP, port forwarding, or requesting a public IP. Multiple routers usually worsen NAT.
consent.text: This site may use third-party cookies if ads or analytics are enabled. Please read the privacy policy.
nat.note.open: Public host address detected without STUN mapping.
nat.note.unknown: No STUN mapping. UDP may be blocked or restricted by the browser.
nat.note.symmetric: Different STUN servers returned different mapped ports.
nat.note.cone: Browser-only tests cannot reliably distinguish NAT2 from NAT3.

contact.intro: For feedback, partnerships, or privacy inquiries, reach us at:
contact.subject: Suggested subject: NAT feedback / Ads compliance / Feature request
contact.note: Please replace this with your actual contact info.
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: This page describes how the NAT Online Check handles information.
privacy.section1.title: 1. Data We Process
privacy.section1.item1: The tool uses WebRTC and public STUN servers in your browser to retrieve mapping info.
privacy.section1.item2: No account is required. We do not collect names, emails, or phone numbers.
privacy.section1.item3: By default, results are not stored on any server.
privacy.section2.title: 2. How It Is Used
privacy.section2.item1: Results are displayed locally in the page.
privacy.section2.item2: You may copy results manually via your browser.
privacy.section3.title: 3. Third-party Services
privacy.section3.item1: The test contacts public STUN servers (e.g., Google, Cloudflare, Twilio). They may log basic network metadata per their policies.
privacy.section3.item2: If ads or analytics are enabled in the future, this page will be updated accordingly.
privacy.section4.title: 4. Cookies & Consent
privacy.section4.body: This site does not set cookies by default. If ads/analytics are enabled, consent mechanisms will be provided where required.
privacy.section5.title: 5. Contact
privacy.contact.prefix: For privacy questions, use the contact page or email
terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.intro: By using this tool, you agree to the terms below.
terms.section1.title: 1. Service Nature
terms.section1.item1: This tool provides network diagnostics for reference only.
terms.section1.item2: Results may vary due to network conditions and browser limitations.
terms.section2.title: 2. User Responsibility
terms.section2.item1: Do not use this tool for illegal or infringing activities.
terms.section2.item2: Do not abuse results to scan or harass others.
terms.section3.title: 3. Disclaimer
terms.section3.item1: We are not liable for any direct or indirect damages arising from use.
terms.section3.item2: Availability of third-party STUN services is controlled by their providers.
terms.section4.title: 4. Updates
terms.section4.body: We may update these terms and will announce changes on this page.
terms.section5.title: 5. Contact
guide.title: How to Improve Your NAT Type
guide.intro: Try these actions in order. Results depend on your network and ISP.
guide.checklist.title: Quick Checklist
guide.checklist.item1: Avoid double NAT (modem + router both doing NAT).
guide.checklist.item3: Use port forwarding or DMZ (with caution).
guide.checklist.item4: Bridge your modem and let the router dial directly.
guide.checklist.item5: Request a public IPv4 or enable IPv6 from your ISP.
guide.steps.title: Suggested Steps
guide.steps.s1.title: 1. Check for Double NAT
guide.steps.s1.body: If both modem and router are doing NAT, good NAT types are hard to achieve. Bridge the modem if possible.
guide.steps.s2.title: 2. Enable UPnP
guide.steps.s2.body: UPnP allows devices to request port mappings automatically. Re-test after enabling.
guide.steps.s3.title: 3. Manual Port Forwarding
guide.steps.s3.body: Forward ports for specific games/apps and keep a fixed local IP for the device.
guide.steps.s4.title: 4. Use DMZ (Caution)
guide.steps.s4.body: DMZ maps all ports to one device. It can improve connectivity but increases risk.
guide.steps.s5.title: 5. Request Public IP / IPv6
guide.steps.s5.body: Under CGNAT, you may need to ask your ISP for a public IPv4. IPv6 can also help.
guide.steps.s6.title: 6. Device & Network Tweaks
guide.steps.s6.body: Updating firmware, easing strict firewall rules, or tuning QoS can improve results.
guide.notes.title: Notes
guide.notes.item1: ISP policies vary widely. Always rely on real test results.
guide.notes.item2: Symmetric NAT is the hardest to improve without ISP support.
guide.prep.title: Before You Start
guide.prep.item1: Run the test to see your current NAT type and public IP.
guide.prep.item2: Check for double NAT (modem + router both routing).
guide.prep.item3: Identify CGNAT: public IP not on your router and port mapping does not work.
guide.steps.s1.item1: Bridge the modem and let the router handle PPPoE.
guide.steps.s1.item2: If bridging is not possible, set the router to AP mode to remove one NAT layer.
guide.steps.s2.item1: After enabling, reboot router and app, then retest.
guide.steps.s2.item2: If your router supports “secure UPnP”, enable it.
guide.steps.s3.item1: Bind a static DHCP address for the device.
guide.steps.s3.item2: Forward only the ports required by the app’s official docs.
guide.steps.s4.item1: Use only temporarily and disable after testing.
guide.steps.s4.item2: Keep host firewall and updates enabled.
guide.steps.s5.item1: Ask for “public IPv4” or a business line.
guide.steps.s5.item2: IPv6 can bypass some NAT limits if the app supports it.
guide.steps.s6.item1: Update router firmware for better NAT compatibility.
guide.platform.title: Platform Checks
guide.platform.item1: PS5 / Xbox / Switch: check NAT type in network settings.
guide.platform.item2: Windows PC: test here and also use in‑app network diagnostics.
guide.platform.item3: Router: verify WAN has a public IP and UPnP is on.
guide.router.title: Router Setup Tips
guide.router.item1: Enable UPnP and avoid conflicting port rules.
guide.router.item2: Bind static DHCP before port forwarding.
guide.router.item3: Bridge if possible; otherwise use AP mode.
guide.router.item4: Overly strict firewall rules can block inbound replies.
guide.isp.title: ISP Limitations
guide.isp.item1: Under CGNAT, NAT type is hard to improve reliably.
guide.isp.item2: Some ISPs offer paid public IPv4.
guide.isp.item3: If IPv6 is available, it is often a better option.
guide.security.title: Security Notes
guide.security.item1: DMZ or wide port opening increases exposure.
guide.security.item2: Open only what you need and keep systems updated.
